speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about life on Mauritius.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after a while, we got organized. Many people-- first of all, I must say, in the beginning we had a-- a terrible typhus epidemic. Because I guess it was a reaction of what had happened, you know, the trip and the disappointment of being sent there. And I guess we also didn't get mosquito nets in time. So as a result, many people got malaria. Because for about a week or so, we didn't have mosquito nets. And that is an absolute necessity, because there are millions of these things around. And I guess as a reaction of the-- of the lack of [SIGHS] cleanliness on-- on board of the ship, the typhus epidemic broke out in a delayed kind of-- as a delayed action. Actually, some people had died already on the ship, but nobody knew about it. Anyway, the fact is, it broke out in Mauritius. And within about three weeks 40 people died, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother, only my mother. I was not sick. I eventually got malaria. Eventually, after a few years, I had malaria. My father never had it, and my mother had malaria and typhus at the same time in the very beginning. And then she didn't-- she survived, somehow. She-- she was very ill, but she survived. My father, who never got anything-- he's very-- was a very tough, tough man. And I didn't get anything, except I think I got eventually malaria. But it wasn't too bad. I went to the hospital a few times, because it was very nasty. And it had a few nasty attacks. But it wasn't-- wasn't-- too bad. Well, life in-- life in general started to settle down. But let me explain to you, first of all, how life was. There was the situation where the men were in the men's camp, the women were in the women's camp. No, there were many, many, married couples with children and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how was-- first of all, how was it going to be organized that the men and women got together? Now, in the-- in the very beginning, for the first few months, it was-- the-- the regulation was that in the afternoon every day, for a certain number of hours, I think in the afternoon-- that changed from time to time-- men and women could get together in an open area adjacent to the camp. OK. But it was an open area, like a-- like a garden. You know, like a vegetable garden. But you could sit there on the grass and chat. And then there was a-- then the police whistled and the men had to go back to the men's camp, women to the women's camp. But eventually, especially the married people, complained to the-- to the-- to the management-- not to the management, to the commandant-- that said, after all, look, listen, what-- what about the sex life? You see what I mean? But-- because what happened was that after a while, during these afternoons, people used to put up tents. And all kinds of things were going on in these tents. And the British are very prude-- prude-- prudish, prudish yeah? Prudish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they didn't like what was going on in these tents. It's not very nice, and so on and so forth. So our-- our-- our leaders said, look, it's your fault. Something has to be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: So various things were proposed, a camp for married people only and another camp for unmarried men and the third camp for unmarried women. This was rejected, because we had now two camps. Now, we had to go to three camps and people are even more separated. Then eventually, what came-- what was-- was finally agreed on between our-- our people and-- and the camp commandant was as follows. Since the men's camp was a prison where every man had a cell-- which was, you could close the door. The cell doors were not locked, by the way. You couldn't leave the camp. But within the camp, you were free to do whatever you want, all right? So, actually, every man had their privacy, right? So what was done was that those women who were married and who had a husband in the camp, they got a passport for only certain hours during the day. But never during the night, because it is indecent to stay with your husband during the night. It's indecent. You can only do that during the day-- during daylight hours. So during the day, they were allowed to visit the men's camp and visit-- they had to go back-- these-- these times changed from time to time. They were eventually extended I think until about 10:00 in the evening, but never more than that and never overnight. For the rest of the period, as far as I know, it was never allowed-- it was never permitted for women to stay overnight in the men's camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what happened [LAUGHS] once was we had a radio in the camp. And the radio was tuned into the BBC from London. And, of course, since the language in the camp was mostly German, we were only allowed to-- to-- to listen to the German language broadcasts of the BBC London. 'Here, East London,' [SINGING] bah, bah, bah, bah bah bah. That was every day. There was a news bulletin at 8 o'clock, and at 10 o'clock, and so forth. And so we had-- there were loud speakers at certain parts in the camp. But there was one central radio room where there was the radio. And there was also a gramophone where we had some records, where we could play some records for entertainment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was at one time, a ship was sunk by a U-boat in the Indian Ocean. And there was a rumor amongst the native population that we have a radio transmitter in the camp and we had been in touch with enemy U-boats. And because we are German speaking-- these people didn't know about Jews and stuff-- we are German agents, you know, who are in touch with the Germans, and we have a radio in the camp. But, of course, we had only a receiver, not the transmitter, you understand? So-- so-- but the British had to do something about this rumor. So what they did-- they-- there was-- there were never any roll reports, because you couldn't escape there anyway, right? Even if you were-- even if you went over the wall and into the-- to outside, you can't escape from that island. Once in a month a ship comes, and so forth. It's impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they did a roll call one fine day, in the middle of the night. In the middle of the night, the-- the British police came. And there was a general roll call. And they found a few women in the men's camp who had stayed with their husbands illegally. So they were punished. I think their passport was withdrawn for about a month, or something like this. So that was the only time that women were allowed-- stayed overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: But life became more normal. How should I say normal? We had a school. A school was established for school children. We had a number of intelligent people who spoke many languages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were professors, and doctors, and Jewish middle class people, all kinds of people who were giving courses. I start-- took Hebrew courses and English. Of course, I studied English, because that's where I basically started to learn English in the camp. I start to-- we were eventually [? able-- ?] able to establish a library, a big library, quite a big library. We got a-- books were sent to us from South Africa. The Jewish community from South Africa was the closest one, so they sent us a whole library and some money, also. Donations were made. But nobody was ever allowed to visit us. They tried to visit. It was never allowed to-- nobody was ever-- from the Jewish agency or Zionist organizations was not allowed to visit the camp. There was even a case where somebody-- somebody's son, who was a-- on the-- in the Merchant Navy, or something, he was in Mauritius. And he want to visit his mother. He was only allowed to visit his mother in the front office. He was-- she was brought to the front office. And he was brought in, so he was able to visit his mother there. And then he had to leave again and that it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very strict. And also, the British said we are never allowed to go back to Palestine. That was it, since we had broken the regulations. After the war, we would be sent back where we came from. Because the war-- they wanted to win the war-- the British, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we will go back to Austria, and to Germany, and to Poland, wherever we came from-- Czechoslovakia-- and that was it. So in the meantime, of course, we were not cut off from the whole world we-- because we-- newspapers were sent to us, you see, from Palestine, from England, from South Africa. So we knew what was going on. We knew all about the Holocaust. Because we got the newspapers from Palestine. And we got the from London and from other papers. So we knew everything that was going on. We knew. And on top of it, as you-- as you-- I've have showed you before, we got even some Red Cross letters from enemy territories. That took a long time to reach Mauritius. It took about six or eight months. Even an ordinary letter from London, England, or from-- from Palestine, to go to Mauritius took about two, three months. A letter, because there was no air mail. It all had to go by ordinary boat and to go through the censorship and all of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took a long time. So in other words, we were in a remote place, which was the thing that bothers us most. Because we're in this remote place cut off from-- from everywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we tried to-- we tried to entertain ourselves. We had schools. We had our own football team. You name it, we got it. We had coffee houses. We had theater groups. We had several theater groups. We had a-- we had a music-- we had a music band called The [INAUDIBLE] Boys, The [INAUDIBLE]. There was a big scandal at one time. One of the [INAUDIBLE] Boys got one of the-- got one-- a British girl from high society, got her pregnant. And he had to leave the-- he had to leave the band as a result of that. He was a very good looking fellow. And they fell in love, and she got pregnant. It was a terrible scandal. Terrible scandal, I'll tell you. And he had to leave the band. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on Mauritius?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I left Mauritius in March of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened was that during the war, the Zionist authorities approached or appealed to the British to allow a Jewish division to be formed-- to be formed to fight against the Nazis. Well, the-- the British didn't want to do that for political reasons. Because they figured-- I don't know. Didn't want. But eventually, Mr. Churchill, I think, was persuaded to-- to-- to agree to that. And eventually, I think in 1944 or so-- I'm not sure about the date-- the Jewish Brigade Group was formed. Now, there were 30,000 Jews had-- had enlisted from Palestine. And they were spread over many units, all kinds of small Jewish units. A company here, and a-- and a battalion here, but they were all in different formations. Many were in the Eighth Army that were fighting in North Africa against Rommel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was all in the British Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was all part of the British army. Oh yes, yes, yes. And then eventually, one fine day, a telegram arrived from the Jewish agency that volunteers from the camp are allowed to join the Jewish brigade group. It was a great honor. Because, for instance, Jews from England were not allowed, or from America, or from-- from anywhere else were not allowed to join them the Jewish Brigade Group, only from Palestine and from our camp. Oh, by the way, in the meantime, since we had a number of Czech citizens and Polish citizens, they were allowed-- about 80 Czech citizens were allowed to join the Czech Army. And about 40 or 50 Polish citizens were allowed to join the Polish army. And they left Mauritius in-- in several groups. But since-- since, for instance, I was an Austrian citizen. Like an enemy, alien, I wasn't allowed to-- to join the British Army. Except, a few left also to join the British Pioneer Corps. There were about 20 or 30 who joined the British Pioneer Corps, Austrians, Jewish. Everyone was Jewish in the camp practically.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I didn't want to join the-- the Pioneer Corps as an Austrian. But when-- when this thing came in at the end of 1944, I volunteered together with about 40 or 50 other people. And eventually, I signed. And I found the thing here, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found the document here. I signed, officially, the declaration on February the 1, 1945. By that time, it was already [LAUGHS] very late in the war. And I left shortly thereafter. And, of course, the rest of the-- my parents, stayed in the camp. I left together with these other 40 people on a military transport, Mauritius for East Africa. We went from there to Kenya. And in Kenya, we stayed for about two weeks, and then we left from there with another ship for the Middle East. Now, that was now in the spring of 1945. The Jewish Brigade Group at that time was in Italy, was fighting in Italy as part of the Eighth Army somewhere in Italy north of Rome, somewhere near Bologna. I don't know exactly. But, of course, we had to go to-- to Egypt. Near the Suez Canal zone, there was the-- the Jewish Brigade Group [? that ?] depot and training center. So we arrived there in the Middle East in the- in the Suez Canal zone somewhere in March. And we went into-- into-- into basic-- what is called basic training. And we went through that basic training, which takes about two or three months. Now, by that time this was already in May of 1945. And on May 8 of 1945-- I think, May 8, I think it was-- there war came to an end. Germany surrendered. OK. Shortly thereafter, the British government decided that the people in Mauritius were allowed to go after all, if they wanted to, to go to Palestine. In other words, anyone who was in the camp of Mauritians had a choice. They had a choice. Either they could go back to-- go to Palestine. Or, if they wanted, they could go back to the country where they came from. In other words, some people decided to go back to Vienna. There was, for instance, in Mauritius, a group of about 30 or 40 Jewish communists. These people were more communist than Jews. They had never known, even, that they were Jews. But under the Hitler laws, they-- they-- suddenly discovered themselves as Jews. But they were-- they were communists. And they wanted to go back to Vienna. And they did. They want to build a communist state in Austria. So they decided to go back to Vienna. And a few, a few, about 30 or 40, went back to Czechoslovakia. And even one or two went back to Germany. I know one back-- one back-- went back to Germany, one or two. But the great majority, including my parents, didn't want to go back. About 95% of the people in Mauritius decided to go-- wanted to go to Palestine, including my parents. So in May-- I think, in May-- they all packed up their-- their things and went back to Palestine. In the meantime, I was in the Middle East in the Suez Canal zone doing the basic training. But my parents went back to Palestine. That was in May or the beginning of June of 1945. So my parents went to Palestine, and that was very nice. They got one month's leave. In other words, there was-- an appeal was made to the Jewish population. Anybody who has not got any relatives could be-- could stay with a family-- with a Jewish family in Palestine. My parents stayed with a Jewish family in a place called-- called Pardes Hanna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardes Hanna, you know the place? OK. My parents got a little house there. They had a house.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had an orange grove, these people. And they had a little house there. And my parents got a room in that house. And there they stayed for about a month. And I was allowed to visit them there. So we got a one week vacation, because they-- they knew that they were in Palestine. So I got there and met my parents. And then after that, my parents got an apartment near Haifa. Located by the-- not a very nice-- a not very-- it was a one-room apartment. One room, a small-- a small room-- it was much smaller than this, let me tell you. It was about this-- this size, [LAUGHS] this size and-- and a very tiny kitchen and a bathroom that had to be shared with somebody else. It was in the middle of nowhere in a place called Kiryat Haim. Maybe you know the place. It's near Haifa in the Kiryat just outside Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, Mr. Wellisch, how you felt when you met your parents in Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I felt fine. You know? I mean, [LAUGHTER] I hadn't-- you know, I had left them only a few months before. You know I mean? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but it was different situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very nice, you know, under the circumstances of course. And I was glad that they-- that they had been said to receive such a wonderful reception, you know. After all, you know, after all these-- these years, they were taken care of, you know? It was very nice that they had this family who-- I was-- yeah, I was very touched, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue with the Jewish Brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, what happened was-- so by-- by-- by May of 19-- 1945, the war was over. The Jewish Brigade Group-- when the war was finished, the Jewish Brigade was still in northern Italy when the war-- when Germany surrendered. Now, it was part of the Jewish-- of the British Army. Now, the British Army at that time occupied part of Germany, right? But for political reasons, the British did not want to send the Jewish Brigade Group as an occupation force to Germany. Oh, by the way, the Jewish Brigade Group danced the hora in Rome around the Titus-- around the Titus arch. Somebody told me. I wasn't there, but somebody told me that. [LAUGHS] You know, as a little thing. The Jewish Brigade Group was about 6,000 or 7,000 people. It's like a small division with-- with artillery and so forth and so on. I was eventually allocated to the-- to the engineering corps. The Jewish Brigade was stationed in Holland and Belgium. First, I went to Antwerp. I still have a passport, which I was there in Antwerp. It was a few months. Then I was transferred to another unit, all within the Jewish Brigade Group, to Eindhoven. And then finally, I was sent to Breda, which is also in Holland, a small town in Holland. And then somebody said to me-- you know, it was very boring. It was so boring. You cannot imagine how boring it was-- had nothing to do. Because what you do there in army, in peace time, is the most boring thing that you can imagine. The only thing that we did was we tried to smuggle refugees to Palestine. That was our main function at that time. That was the only thing we really did. And we had all the-- we-- we faked papers. And we supplied people with fake papers and fake uniforms, and sent them as fake Jewish soldiers on leave to Palestine and all kinds of other things. Sometimes they were caught, then they were sent back. Then they were sent again. So there was a continuous traffic.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you continue on in the Jewish Brigade Group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what happened was, on fine day somebody said to me, you know, what are you doing here anyway? Your parents are elderly people. You really have to look after your parents. You should ask for a home posting.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what do you mean home posting? I'm here with the Jewish Brigade. He says, no, you're actually part of the British Army. You could ask. There is such a thing as a home posting, because you want to be near your parents. You have to look after parents, especially an only child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, I-- I tell you, you go to this particular place, you fill in all these forms, and you put it in. See what happens. I did. And like magic, within two weeks [LAUGHS] I was transferred back to Palestine. Now, in Palestine there was a very serious situation. In Palestine, in 1946, there was this-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. They were-- they were-- they were conducting a terrorist campaign against the British. They were blowing up bridges and so forth. There was a lot of bad feeling between the British Army and the Jewish population of Palestine. Because-- because these underground groups-- the Stern Gang, and the Stern Group or whatever you call them, and the [NON-ENGLISH] were conducting this-- this campaign. Now, I was-- I was sent there as a Jewish soldier as being part of the British-- British Army. So they didn't-- I arrived there in this British camp in [INAUDIBLE], which was a central British depot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- I arrived. Just then, there was an attack on the camp by the terrorists, a terrorist attack. Sirens were going. It was shooting and so forth. And they looked at me as if I were a spy. You see what I mean? So anyway-- but they had to-- but they were fair, nothing anti-Semitic or anything. They were very fair. They said, look, where do your parents live? I said, my parents are living in Kiryat Haim, near Haifa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they looked up the list. You are in the Royal Army Service Corps. Well, he says, yes, we have a place for you. There is a field bakery not far from Kiryat Haim. And we'll post you to the field bakery. And you'll be-- but I said, I'm not a field baker. I'm not a baker. I'm-- I'm-- I'm nothing. You know, I'm-- I'm not-- But my ranking in the Royal Army Service Corps-- your rank is not-- my rank was-- was driver. Now, I'm not a-- at that time, I didn't know how to drive a car. But a rank is driver. Like private in the Army, in the Royal Army Service Corps, you're a driver. You can still see that on my-- on some of my papers. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me to this field bakery. So they arrive at the field bakery, which is in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE]. You know where the oil refinery is? It was not far from the oil refinery, about 15 minutes by bus from where my parents were, in Kiryat Haim. Which was very nice. So I arrived at the bakery. Ah, the driver has arrived. But I said, I'm not a driver. [LAUGHS] I said, it's my rank. Well, he said, what are you doing here then? We don't-- we need a driver. We don't need a rank-- we need a driver driver. Because we drive the truck. But I said, I'm not a driver. Well, what shall we do with you? I said, [LAUGHS] that's not-- that's not my business. It's your business.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so they didn't know what to do with me, so I didn't do anything. So they gave me a pass. But they-- but since I'm not a baker, they couldn't send me to the bakery. Because they are not a baker. And since I'm not a driver-- they didn't want to give me a rifle either to do guard duty, because the camp has to be guarded. Yeah. Every military-- they didn't want to do that either, because of the political situation with the Jewish attacks on these camps. So they-- so I didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I did was I got a permanent pass that I slept at my parents' home. In the morning, I arrived at the camp, had breakfast. Then I went into the canteen, and I read a book. Then I had lunch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after lunch, I-- I was sitting in the afternoon until about 4:00 or 5:00, 4 'o'clock, then I had an early supper. And then I left again. And then I did-- and that I did for several months until I was discharged in, I think, September of 1946. I was discharged, I went back to [INAUDIBLE], I got a complete discharge out-- a suit, tie-- tie, a shirt-- everything, a pair of shoes and everything, a hat and everything. And I was discharged from the British Army in, I think, September or October, 19-- 1946. And that was my service with the-- with the British Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I joined my parents. Now, in the meantime, my father had got a job with the-- in the British Army camp as a-- as a stock keeper. He didn't speak one word of English, but he still got the job as a stock. Because somebody else got him the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- what he did there, I have no idea. But he got a job, which was paid Which it paid very little, but it's better than nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father was a businessman. He couldn't find anything else to do. He didn't speak Hebrew. He spoke only German and Hungarian, of course. It's very important, you know. What you're going to do in Israel with speaking in German and Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so I arrived there and lived with my parents. We were all living in one room, which was smaller-- I would say about this size. The room was so small that we had a pull-out bed. We had one bed. And under this bed, you could pull out the other two beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because, otherwise, you couldn't move around. And our table, the table that we used was a folding table. Because, otherwise, there was not enough table-- not enough space. Only when we had lunch, we used to unfold the table, so we had lunch, [LAUGHS] you know? Then we had to fold it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we had a-- we had a wardrobe. Over there, there are no built-in wardrobes. You have to have wardrobe with the three-- where we had our things. [LAUGHS] You know, it's-- it's really funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till, I think, '51. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the Israeli Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now, let me say, let me say-- after, I had to do some work, right? I had to make a living. So in the camp in Mauritius-- I hadn't-- I didn't go into that, OK? I think I told you that I was-- in Vienna, I started out as a-- as a cabinet maker apprentice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the camp in Mauritius, there was a cabinet-- a carpenter or cabinet making shop. You had to do something. Otherwise, you go mad. So everybody did-- did something. So I went to-- I got a -- a job. I worked at the-- at the cabinet making shop doing work for work within the camp let's say, repairing beds and things like that. So about over four years, I built up a certain knowledge of cabinet making and carpentry. So when I arrived in-- in Palestine, I had a quite a certain knowledge of this trade. Not that great, but even so I knew quite a bit. And I had a cousin there on my mother's side who was an-- an architect interior decorator. And he knew a number of cabinet makers. And he got me a job with one of these cabinet makers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did some work. And there I learned, very quickly, some more in this field. And we did-- mostly, we did custom-made furniture. That's what we did, very fine furniture, fine veneered furniture. We get our own veneering. And, you know, custom made furniture-- let's say somebody wants to have a dining room set. We did them a dining room set. It was designed specifically for this particular person, you know, the size and everything, or a bedroom set, or whatever, a built-in cupboard and so forth. That's what-- that's what we did. And eventually, this man gave up the business. And we took over the-- the employees took over the firm as a co-operative. So there were about four or five people in the co-operative.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop you there please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.
